evidence_basis_summary: Authors fix the analysis universe to the 200 Austrian immigration statements from Ennser-Jedenastik and Meyer (2018) and later 200 sampled from Haselmayer and Jenny (2017), but the case is not about adjudicating which materials belong in scope.
notes: upgraded low to medium: corpus selection is described but is not the central role under test

evidence_basis_summary: Authors constrain the causal interpretation of LLM behavior to a token-embedding / party-cue heuristic mechanism and test alternative prompts, temperatures, and a less-polarized corpus to rule out rival explanations.
notes: upgraded low to medium and flipped observable no to yes: paper explicitly polices mechanism claims (embedding story) via prompt, temperature, and alternative-corpus robustness checks

evidence_basis_summary: Authors reuse the human codebook's explicit definitions of positive/negative/neutral stance on immigration and retain LLM-vs-human disagreement as a substantive finding rather than collapsing it.
notes: upgraded medium to high: full text directly concerns construct boundaries and visible disagreement

evidence_basis_summary: Authors deliberately add a second corpus (12,329 Austrian parliamentary and press-release statements covering less polarized domains) to stop the evidence base from collapsing onto the immigration corpus.
notes: upgraded low to high and flipped observable no to yes: second corpus (Haselmayer and Jenny 2017) is explicitly introduced to check whether findings depend on a single politically salient domain

evidence_basis_summary: Authors design the full annotation protocol: identical prompts to human instructions, fresh client instances, temperature=0, ten replications per run, majority-rule adjudication, and alternative-prompt and temperature robustness runs.
notes: upgraded low to high and flipped observable partial to yes: protocol design is extensively documented in Replication setup section

evidence_basis_summary: Authors explicitly test whether the party-cue effect transports from the high-salience immigration corpus to a less polarized parliamentary-debate corpus and find party cues have no differentiating effect there.
notes: upgraded low to high and flipped observable no to yes: Appendix G transportability test is a central finding

evidence_basis_summary: The design is offline text annotation of archival manifesto and parliamentary statements with no interviews, fieldwork, or interactive elicitation.
notes: upgraded low to high: confidently not applicable given the annotation-only design

evidence_basis_summary: Discussion warns researchers about unknown priors in LLM annotators and frames implications for who is answerable when LLMs are deployed as coders, but does not allocate authorship or disclosure regimes.
notes: upgraded low to medium and flipped observable no to partial: conclusion raises accountability concerns (unknown priors, inability to inspect training data) without formal allocation rules

evidence_basis_summary: The coding task hinges on the authors' explicit definitions of positive and negative immigration statements (with worked examples) that humans impose on both LLMs and human coders.
notes: upgraded medium to high: full-text confirmation of codebook-style construct boundary work

evidence_basis_summary: Authors preserve human-coder variance as the benchmark, report Krippendorff's Alpha across ten runs and across LLM-human pairs, and compare LLM and human labeling distributions side by side.
notes: upgraded low to high and flipped observable partial to yes: inter-LLM and inter-coder reliability analyses keep subgroup disagreement visible

evidence_basis_summary: The alternative parliamentary-debate corpus is used explicitly to test whether LLM bias travels across policy salience contexts; the paper finds it does not, conditioning the original result.
notes: upgraded low to high and flipped observable no to yes: split-candidate version mirrors the starting role and is directly supported

evidence_basis_summary: Authors benchmark LLM outputs against the human-coder reference distribution from Ennser-Jedenastik and Meyer (2018) using Krippendorff's Alpha and predicted-probability comparisons.
notes: upgraded low to high and flipped observable no to yes: the whole evaluation is a calibration check against a human reference

evidence_basis_summary: No interviewer or fieldworker is present; the task is automated annotation of pre-existing policy statements.
notes: upgraded low to high: confidently not applicable

evidence_basis_summary: Authors invoke contextual knowledge of Austrian immigration politics (e.g., anti-immigration rhetoric around needs assessments) to explain LLM-human disagreement, but contextual interpretation is illustrative rather than a primary workflow role.
notes: upgraded low to medium and flipped observable no to partial: Table 1 discussion uses tacit Austrian political context to adjudicate disputed labels

evidence_basis_summary: The design is full labeling of every statement, not title/abstract triage or coarse screening, so the screener cluster is not at stake.
notes: upgraded low to high: confidently not applicable

role_label: Routine coder on high-consensus, ground-truth-rich tasks
evidence_basis_summary: The paper's core question is whether LLMs can replace human coders on a ground-truth-anchored sentiment task; it finds LLMs are internally consistent but systematically biased by party cues, so humans must remain as auditors rather than primary coders.
notes: upgraded low to high and flipped observable partial to yes and status unclear to shrinking: the whole paper is about this shrinking task cluster and its limits

evidence_basis_summary: LLMs are used as label-producing annotators, not as drafters of prose; no sentence-level writing task is delegated.
notes: upgraded low to high: confidently not applicable
